content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Adriana Nunez 
Attorney 
State Water Resources Control Board 
Office of Chief Counsel 
1001 l Street, 22nd Floor 
Sacramento, California 9S814 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-16-262 
Dear Ms. Nunez: 
January 19, 2016 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Hidali Garcia, a member of the 
Regional Water Quality Control Board, Colorado River Basin Region (the "Board") regarding the 
conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 This advice applies only to 
the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and no other general conflict of interest prohibitions 
such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. Moreover, this letter is based on the facts 
presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission does not act as a finder of fact when it renders 
advice. (/11 re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) 
QUESTION 
May Ms. Garcia take part in the Board's decision to issue an order permitting the discharge 
of wastes from a slaughterhouse and meat processing facility located 4 ½ miles from her home? 
CONCLUSION 
Yes. It is not reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on 
Ms. Garcia's real property. 
FACTS 
The Board will be considering the adoption of an Order (the "Order") that would permit 
OWB Packers, LLC (the "Applicant") to discharge wastes from a wastewater treatment system that 
will service an existing slaughterhouse and beef processing facility (the "Facility") located at 57 
East Shank Road in the City of Brawley. 
1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in Government Code Sections 8l000 through 910 14. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 181 IO through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-16-262 
Page No. 2 
Other business entities have previously operated the Facility since at least 1995. The Facility 
was most recently operated by National Beef Company ("National Beef') between 2006 and May 
2014 when it was sold to the Applicant. The Facility has been idle since then. National Beef was 
alleged to have violated various provisions of the Clean Water Act and was the subject of multiple 
complaints as the result of nuisance odors. The most recent odor violation occurred in November 
2013. The Applicant was not associated with the Facility before the purchase and National Beef has 
no further involvement with or control over the Facility. 
The Order would establish Waste Discharge Requirements (the "WDRs") that would allow 
the Applicant to operate wastewater ponds and associated waste treatment systems and to discharge 
treated wastewater to agricultural land. Staff has proposed WDRs that would authorize the 
discharge of up to 238,000 gallons per day of treated industrial wastewater into onsite unlined 
ponds for storage and the discharge of reclaimed wastewater for agricultural reuse {crop irrigation) 
on approximately 140 acres of farmland to grow Bermuda grass or other fodder crops for cattle 
feed. 
The proposed WDRs will require implementation of mitigation measures to comply with the 
California Environmental Quality Act {"CEQA") and impose additional requirements necessary to 
protect groundwater and surface water quality and to prevent nuisance. The WDRs will also include 
provisions to ensure that the facility does not cause nuisance odors. 
The Applicant is currently installing an upgraded treatment system that is expected to be 
completed by February 28, 2017 and operations would commence shortly thereafter, provided the 
WDRs are issued. The upgrades consist of a BioFiltro BIDA® System {"BioFiltro") that would 
replace the anaerobic and aerated pond-based wastewater treatment systems. The BioFiltro is a 
patented treatment system that uses biological processes to treat wastewater in concrete 
containment tanks. 
The Imperial County Air Pollution Control District (the "County Air Pollution Control 
District") has already issued a permit to construct and operate the new wastewater treatment system. 
The air quality permit "addresses the operation of the pond-based wastewater treatment system by 
prohibiting its operation from causing a nuisance" its rules. The rules prohibit the discharge of air 
contaminants or other material "which cause injury, detriment, nuisance or annoyance to any 
considerable number of persons or to the public or which endanger the comfort, repose, health or 
safety of any such persons or the public or which cause or have a natural tendency to cause injury or 
damage to business or property." 
The WDRs will also include requirements that prohibit nuisance odors. In compliance with 
CEQA, the WDRs must, at a minimum, include mitigation measures MM-AIR-4 (prohibit nuisance 
odors) and MM-AIR-5 (monitor dissolved oxygen content of ponds to prevent odors). 
The Facility covers two parcels located at 57 East Shank Road in the northern part of 
Brawley and is south of the State Route 78/111 Brawley Bypass, north of the Brawley Municipal 
Airport, east of Southern Pacific Railroad tracks, and west of agricultural fields. 


File No. A-16-262 
Page No. 3 
Ms. Garcia owns and occupies residential property located approximately 4 ½ miles 
southwest of 57 E. Shank Road. Her home was affected by nuisance odors from the Facility while it 
was operated by National Beef. Although Ms. Garcia could not smell the odors inside her home, the 
odors were sometimes present in her backyard. You state that the odors did not impede outdoor 
activities. 
The Applicant has advised the Board's staff that the Facility upgrades, abandonment of the 
onsite treatment ponds, and changing the discharge to the agricultural fields at agronomic rates will 
eliminate nuisance odors. You have not identified any other effects on Ms. Garcia's property from 
the operation of the Facility. Ms. Garcia does not own any interest in nor receive income from the 
Applicant. 
The Board adopted the Initial Study and Mitigated Negative Declaration and the Issuance of 
Waste Discharge Requirements for the Agricultural Beneficial Reuse of Treated Industrial 
Wastewater, 57 East Shank Road in Brawley, California ("IS/MND") in November 2016, finding 
that the proposed system and mitigation measures would cause any odors from the facility to be less 
than significant. Ms. Garcia did not participate in the Board's consideration of the IS/MND. 
The Board will be considering staff recommendations for the Waste Discharge 
Requirements to be included in the proposed Order. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using 
his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial 
interest.2 A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision, within the meaning 
of'the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable3 that the decision will have a material financial effect on 
one or more of the public official's interests as set forth in Section 87103. 
You have identified one interest related to the subject decision, Ms. Garcia's personal 
residence, which is located approximately 4 ½ miles from the Facility. Regulation l 8705.2(a) 
provides a list of circumstances under which the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a 
governmental decision on real property in which an official has a financial interest is material. As 
relevant to the facts here, the financial effect will be material if the decision: 
• Regulation 18705.2(a)(IO): "Would change the character of the parcel ofreal 
property by substantially altering traffic levels or intensity of use, including parking, 
of property surrounding the official's real property parcel, the view, privacy, noise; 
1 When a public official who holds an office specified in Section 87200 has a conflict ofinterest in a decision 
noticed at a public meeting, he or she must: (I) immediately prior to the discussion of the item, orally identify each type 
of interest involved in the decision as well as details of the interest as discussed in Regulation 18 702.S(b ), on the record 
of the meeting; (2) recuse himself or herself; and (3) leave the room for the duration of the discussion and/or vote on the 
item. 
1 A financial effect need not be likely to be considered reasonably foreseeable. In general, if the financial effect 
can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable. If the 
financial result cannot be expected absent extraordinary circumstances not subject to the public official's control, it is 
not reasonably foreseeable. 


File No. A-16-262 
Page No. 4 
• Regulation 18705.2(a){l2): "Would cause a reasonably prudent person, using due 
care and consideration under the circumstances, to believe that the governmental 
decision was of such a nature·that its reasonably foreseeable effect would influence 
the market value of the official's property." 
You state that the most recent odor violation occurred under prior ownership in November 
2013, the Facility ceased operations in May 2014, and there have been no wastewater odors 
emanating from the Facility since then. Therefore, any negative effects on Ms. Garcia's property 
caused by odors from the Facility ceased nearly three years ago. 
The Applicant, as the new owner of the Facility, has been issued a permit from the County 
Air Pollution Control District to construct and operate the new wastewater treatment system. The 
terms of the permit prohibit the Applicant from discharging air contaminants or other material 
"which cause injury, detriment, nuisance or annoyance to any considerable number of persons or to 
the public or which endanger the comfort, repose, health or safety of any such persons or the public 
or which cause or have a natural tendency to cause injury or damage to business or property." Also, 
the proposed WDRs will require implementation of mitigation measures "to ensure that the Facility 
does not cause nuisance odors." Currently, the idle Facility does not generate waste from which 
odors may emanate and, once operations resume, any such odors would be eliminated by the new 
wastewater treatment system. 
You have not identified any other effects that the re-opening of the Facility would have on 
Ms. Garcia's property nor provided any facts suggesting that there might be other potential uses for 
the site. Under these facts, including the documents you have provided, we do not believe that a 
decision to issue the Order will affect the value of Ms. Garcia's real property. Therefore, the 
decision will not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on her real property and she 
may participate in the decision. 
VJ:jgl 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at {916) 322-5660. 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
General Counsel 
By: 
Valentina Joyce 
Counsel, Legal Division

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: May Ms. Garcia take part in the Board's decision to issue an order permitting the discharge 
of wastes from a slaughterhouse and meat processing facility located 4 ½ miles from her home?

CONCLUSION: Yes. It is not reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on 
Ms. Garcia's real property.
